stack.rationale: Native drag-and-drop, security-scoped file-system access, and a responsive live-preview table are core to this class; SwiftUI delivers them natively where a web wrapper would fight the sandbox and the file APIs.

uxStructure: single window: a file table (original → new name, changed parts highlighted), a rule editor, and an Apply button
states: empty (drop files or pick a folder), preview (live-updated), applied (with Undo available)
live preview recomputes on every keystroke; nothing on disk changes until Apply

dataModel.storage: in-memory file list + rules; UserDefaults for recent rule presets

capabilities: user-selected file-system access via NSOpenPanel + security-scoped bookmarks

errorHandling: conflict detection blocks Apply when two files would map to the same name
an undo stack restores all original names after an applied batch
preview is pure — it never mutates files
testPlan: loading 10 files and typing a find/replace shows live-updated names with changes highlighted, and no file is modified yet
applying a batch then choosing Undo restores every original name
a rule mapping two files to the same name is flagged and blocked before Apply

build.signing: Developer ID + notarization (required for a sandboxed file-access app)

decisionLog.selectionCriteria: native drag-drop, sandboxed file access, responsive preview table
decisionLog.rationale: native file APIs and a fast preview table beat a web wrapper here

decisionLog.selectionCriteria: user must see every change before any file is touched
decisionLog.rationale: preview-before-apply is the safety-critical market standard

decisionLog.selectionCriteria: operates directly on files; only presets need persistence
decisionLog.rationale: no need to persist file state, only reusable rules

decisionLog.selectionCriteria: least privilege for a file-touching tool
decisionLog.rationale: only the folders the user picks, via NSOpenPanel + bookmarks

options: grant the file entitlement in the manifest now
defer the access scope to confirmation
decisionLog.selectionCriteria: renaming touches user files; scope was flagged for confirmation
decisionLog.chosen: defer the access scope to confirmation
decisionLog.rationale: the contract marked file scope as needs-confirmation; keep it out of the manifest until confirmed

options: explicit Apply gate + undo + conflict detection
decisionLog.selectionCriteria: renaming is destructive and must be reversible
decisionLog.chosen: explicit Apply gate + undo + conflict detection
decisionLog.rationale: safety is the market standard for a destructive file operation

decisionLog.selectionCriteria: a sandboxed file-access app must be notarized to run for users
decisionLog.rationale: required for a sandboxed file-access app

openConfirmations.rationale: renaming touches the user's files; confirm it is limited to user-selected folders only

research.feature: live preview + full undo for batch renames
research.finding: Batchio computes preview as a pure function of the rules and keeps a 100-operation undo stack; conflicts are detected before apply, never after

experiments.hypothesis: security-scoped bookmarks let the app re-access user-selected folders across relaunches
experiments.method: isolated probe: pick a folder via NSOpenPanel, persist a security-scoped bookmark, relaunch, re-resolve and write
experiments.result: the bookmark re-resolves and grants read-write access after relaunch

loggingPlan.why: diagnose failed or partial rename batches
confirm the safety gate actually fired
trace reversions when a user reports lost names
